title: Local vs Global Minimums
concept: A global minimum is the lowest possible point of error, while a local minimum is a deceptive shallow dip that can trap the algorithm early.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What happens if your learning rate is too small?

options.text: The model will take forever to train and may get stuck
The model will overshoot the lowest point of the cost function
The model will train perfectly and extremely quickly

exercises.explain: A learning rate that is too small leads to tiny steps, causing extremely slow training and higher risk of getting stuck.

cards.title: Trapped in a puddle
cards.body: Imagine walking downhill in the dark. You step into a small, muddy puddle and find that every direction out goes uphill. You think you've reached the bottom, but the true valley is actually 500 feet lower.

cards.title: Local vs Global
cards.body: A local minimum is a point where the cost is lower than nearby points but not the absolute lowest. The global minimum is the absolute lowest error possible.

cards.title: Dips and valleys
cards.body: The terrain of a cost function can have many hills and valleys. A local minimum is just a temporary dip in the mountain, while the global minimum is the deepest valley floor.

cards.title: Stuck in a rut
cards.body: If a model gets stuck in a local minimum, it might think it has found the best weights, but a much more accurate set of weights exists elsewhere.

cards.title: Flatness is not a guarantee
cards.body: It's a myth that reaching a flat slope means you have solved the problem. You might have just landed on a flat ledge (called a saddle point) or a shallow ditch.

cards.title: Minimum types
cards.body: The difference between local and global minimum points on a loss curve:

left.text:
A small ditch.
Slope is zero, but error is still relatively high.

right.text:
The deepest valley.
The ultimate destination where error is at its absolute lowest.

exercises.prompt: Why is a local minimum problematic for Gradient Descent?

options.text: It makes the model run out of memory
The slope becomes zero, so the algorithm stops, thinking it has reached the best valley
It increases the learning rate automatically

exercises.explain: Since the slope is flat at a local minimum, the algorithm thinks it reached the bottom and stops, even though a better solution exists.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Every cost function has only one minimum.
exercises.explain: False! Simple linear models often have one minimum, but complex non-linear models can have many local minimums and saddle points.

exercises.sentence: The absolute lowest point of error across the entire cost function is called the ___ minimum.
exercises.explain: The global minimum represents the absolute best configuration for the model, where the error is as low as it can possibly get.

exercises.prompt: Which technique is most likely to help a model escape a local minimum?

options.text: Adding a bit of momentum to the steps so they can push up and out of shallow dips
Decreasing the size of the dataset so the model has less to learn
Setting the learning rate to zero so the weights freeze

exercises.explain: Adding momentum (like a rolling ball) or using learning rate schedules helps the optimizer roll past or jump out of small local dips.

exercises.prompt: You are training a complex image classifier. It reaches 70% accuracy and stops improving. The slope is zero, but you know 95% is possible. What happened?

options.text: The algorithm got trapped in a local minimum or flat region and thinks it has reached the absolute bottom
The computer ran out of processing power to find the global minimum
The model has reached the global minimum, and 95% is mathematically impossible

exercises.explain: Brilliant! The model got trapped in a local minimum, where local slopes are flat but the error is still higher than the global minimum. You've conquered local vs global minimums!
